# Binding Affinity Determination in Drug Design: Insights from Lock and Key, Induced Fit, Conformational Selection, and Inhibitor Trapping Models

### Affiliation

# Binding Affinity Determination in Drug Design: Insights from Lock and Key, Induced Fit, Conformational Selection, and Inhibitor Trapping Models

### Author

### Affiliation

## Abstract

Binding affinity is a fundamental parameter in drug design, describing the strength of the interaction between a molecule and its target protein. Accurately predicting binding affinity is crucial for the rapid development of novel therapeutics, the prioritization of promising candidates, and the optimization of their properties through rational design strategies. Binding affinity is determined by the mechanism of recognition between proteins and ligands. Various models, including the lock and key, induced fit, and conformational selection, have been proposed to explain this recognition process. However, current computational strategies to predict binding affinity, which are based on these models, have yet to produce satisfactory results. This article explores the connection between binding affinity and these protein-ligand interaction models, highlighting that they offer an incomplete picture of the mechanism governing binding affinity. Specifically, current models primarily center on the binding of the ligand and do not address its dissociation. In this context, the concept of ligand trapping is introduced, which models the mechanisms of dissociation. When combined with the current models, this concept can provide a unified theoretical framework that may allow for the accurate determination of the ligands' binding affinity.

**Keywords:**
Abl; RAS; binding affinity; conformational selection; drug design; imatinib; induced fit; inhibitor potency; inhibitor trapping; kinase; lock and key; magic methyl.

![Malvern Panalytical](/resource/img/malvernpanalyticallogo.svg)

# Binding affinity

Label free protein ligand interaction analysis solutions using GCI and ITC

Binding affinity

Understanding binding affinity is key to appreciating the intermolecular interactions driving biological processes, structural biology, and structure-function relationships. Binding affinity is also measured as part of the [drug discovery](/en/industries/pharmaceuticals/drug-discovery "Research and discovery") process to help design drugs that bind their targets selectively and specifically.

## What is binding affinity?

![](https://p3.aprimocdn.net/malvernpanalytical/2af3b8d9-48a0-48ce-a98b-aff800ba4096/placeholder-concentric-rings_Original%20file.svg?quality=60&width=532&crop=2:1,smart)

## Dissociation constant

Binding affinity is the strength of the binding interaction between a single biomolecule (e.g., a protein or DNA) to its ligand or binding partner (e.g., a drug or inhibitor). Binding affinity is typically measured and reported by the [equilibrium dissociation constant](/en/products/measurement-type/dissociation-constant "Dissociation constant") (KD), which is used to evaluate and rank order strengths of bimolecular interactions. The smaller the KD value, the greater the binding affinity of the ligand for its target. The larger the KDvalue, the more weakly the target molecule and ligand are attracted to and bind to one another.

Binding affinity is influenced by non-covalent intermolecular interactions such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic and van der Waals forces between the two molecules. In addition, binding affinity between a ligand and its target molecule may be affected by the presence of other molecules.

![](https://dam.malvernpanalytical.com/9d556535-d7fa-4005-9f4c-ae3600eccae8/Figure%202%20TN200101-Creoptix-binding-unpurified-GPCRs_Original%20file.jpg?quality=60&width=515)

## Quick characterization of binding onto unpurified GPCRs

Technical note

## Why should I measure binding affinity for my application?

Whenever you are characterizing proteins, nucleic acids, compounds or any biomolecule, understanding their binding affinity to substrates, inhibitors, and cofactors is key to appreciating their intermolecular interactions. This understanding is relevant when studying interactions such as enzymatic reactions, protein complexes, or receptor binding.

For drug discovery, binding affinity helps design drugs that bind their targets selectively and specifically.

## Methods for measuring binding affinity

There are many ways to measure binding affinity, including methods that require the interactors to be labeled and label-free approaches. The main labeled, qualitative method (i.e., binding: yes/no) is enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). Key label-free, quantitative methods include spectroscopic assays, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), or [optical biosensors](/en/products/category/optical-biosensors "Optical biosensors") such as surface plasmon resonance (SPR), biolayer interferometry (BLI), and grating-coupled interferometry (GCI).

## Evaluating binding affinity

![](https://p3.aprimocdn.net/malvernpanalytical/2af3b8d9-48a0-48ce-a98b-aff800ba4096/placeholder-concentric-rings_Original%20file.svg?quality=60&width=532&crop=2:1,smart)

01 March 2022 | English

## Kinetics Guide - Binding kinetics with the WAVEsystem

However you measure binding affinity, the measurement will result in multiple report points, from which a binding affinity curve can be created. This curve depends on both the concentration of the sample and the interaction between sample and target.

This makes it important to know the concentration of your sample and consider the right incubation period, in addition to your regular, proper experimental controls. It’s particularly important to reach equilibrium (where the amount of molecules binding to the target is the same as the amount disassociating from the target) during your assay. Without reaching equilibrium you cannot reliably determine affinity, as the binding model cannot be reliably fitted.

Find out more in our Kinetics Guide.

![](https://dam.malvernpanalytical.com/00adb34b-8a67-46c0-a455-ad9e00cbda0e/636217518970921572AD_Original%20file.png?quality=60&width=515)

## Isothermal Titration Calorimetry: Theory and Practice

Whitepaper

## What binding affinity solutions does Malvern Panalytical offer?

Malvern Panalytical offers both [Grating Coupled Interferometry (GCI)](/en/products/technology/grating-coupled-interferometry "Grating-coupled interferometry (GCI)") and [Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC)](/en/products/technology/microcalorimetry/isothermal-titration-calorimetry "Isothermal Titration Calorimetry"). Both are [label-free quantification](/en/campaigns/expand-your-label-free-characterization-analysis "1. Creoptix LFA - introduction") techniques, allowing the use of native molecules. Highly quantitative affinity (KD values) can be derived from both for a broad range of interactions.

GCI is an optical method that can provide real-time measurements of [kinetics](/en/products/measurement-type/binding-kinetics "Binding kinetics") as well as affinity. It measures the change in refractive index in an evanescent field caused by the binding event. GCI measures KD values in the millimolar to picomolar range. In addition, it can determine the kinetics of an interaction – more specifically, the on- and off-rates (ka and kd).

ITC measures the heat change associated with the binding event, measures KD values in the millimolar-to-nanomolar range and determines the binding stoichiometry and binding thermodynamics of the interaction. Both kinetics and thermodynamics are important in the characterization of intermolecular interactions. 

![](https://dam.malvernpanalytical.com/b266ecf3-637f-41d5-a36a-ae29010df43c/GCI_CX_weblandscape_Original%20file.jpg?quality=60&width=532&crop=2:1,smart)

## Grating-coupled interferometry (GCI)

![](https://dam.malvernpanalytical.com/78c6979e-cbed-4445-8505-ad9d0189bdbe/636216663640470571XJ_Original%20file.jpg?quality=60&width=532&crop=2:1,smart)

## Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC)

![](https://dam.malvernpanalytical.com/196a2e35-168d-4d60-95a0-ad9d018a0003/636216665241878117AE_Original%20file.jpg?quality=60&width=532&crop=2:1,smart)

## Label-free interaction analysis

## Is GCI or ITC most suitable for my application?

The affinity from both devices is orthogonal and taken together can provide confidence when a highly quantitative KD is required – for instance, in lead optimization applications.

GCI benefits from higher sensitivity, higher throughput, and lower sample consumption, and performs well with crude samples. If these factors and the kinetic information are the most important to your application, then this is the instrument of choice.

If thermodynamic data (enthalpy and entropy) and stoichiometry are most important, then ITC is the best solution. ITC also benefits from minimal assay development, so can generate results more quickly if only a small number of measurements of a given interaction pair are anticipated. The technique is also non-destructive, and the sample can be recovered post-experiment.

The [WAVEsystem](/en/products/product-range/wave "Creoptix WAVE") (GCI) and [MicroCal PEAQ-ITC](/en/products/product-range/microcal-range/microcal-itc-range/microcal-peaq-itc "MicroCal PEAQ-ITC") (ITC) are both designed with the user in mind and are known for being easy to use in their respective instrument classes.

--- SOURCE 5: Binding Affinity - an overview | ScienceDirect Topics ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/binding-affinity

SUMMARY:
Friedman _et al._ (1994) docked epitopic fragments (heptapeptides) to the binding site of the antipeptide antibody B13I2 (both the free and complexed X-ray structures), using the Metropolis Monte Carlo docking program of Goodsell and Olson (1990). The empirical Gibbs free energy calculations for antibody–antigen (Novotny _et al.,_ 1989; Novotny, 1991; Tulip _et al.,_ 1994) and enzyme–inhibitor (Krystek _et al.,_ 1993) complexes consistently indicated that only a small number of amino acids, approximately 30% of the total contact surface area, contributed actively to binding energetics. The concept offered resolution of an apparent paradox: on the one hand, a multitude of side-chain–side-chain interactions at the interface [∼16 side chains in the antibody as well as in the antigen; see, e.g., Amit _et al._ (1986)] and, on the other hand, the experimentally derived size of the binding site as four to six amino acids (Haber _et al.,_ 1967; Sela, 1969; Schechter, 1971; Kabat, 1970).

Binding Affinity
================

In subject area:[Neuroscience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience)

Binding affinity refers to the strength of interaction between a drug and its target receptor. It is calculated based on the relative affinity of the drug to bind to the site of action compared to other sites, with higher affinity leading to increased selectivity of the drug.

### 10.3.9 Receptor Binding Affinity

Receptor binding affinity of nanoformulations can be calculated by relative binding affinity. This is the portion of the drug that is required to bind to the site of action; for that it must have the highest affinity in contrast to the amount required to accommodate at the site of action for which it has lower affinity. As the ratio of binding increases, the higher the selectivity of the said drug. Given that this approach deals with receptor binding affinity of [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), it includes placing a receptor in a cuvette and labeling it with great affinity radioactive ligand as a drug. Subsequently, the drug of interest whose binding affinity was quantified is added in excess quantity to a sequence of test tubes comprising the same quantity of the tagged site of interest. Whereas, if the quantity of nonlabeled drug was enhanced, it is replaced by the radioactive label tagging. Therefore, % occupancy of the drug at desired sites was attained by means of a certain concentration that is not labeled and could be determined as the decrease in radioactivity during incubation (Lara et al., 2017).

Zhou et al. prepared immunoliposomal nanoparticles for [epidermal growth factor receptor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/epidermal-growth-factor-receptor "Learn more about epidermal growth factor receptor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") targeting by means of a [scFv antibody](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/single-chain-fragment-variable-antibody "Learn more about scFv antibody from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). That modified antibody was compared with parenteral scFv C10 antibody for receptor-mediated targeting. It was found that over C10 scFv modified scFv were found to be 3- to 18-fold enhanced in affinity in A431 [EGFR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/epidermal-growth-factor-receptor "Learn more about EGFR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") overexpressed cells (_K_ _D_=15–88 nM). After incorporating with immunoliposomal nanoparticles, affinity was enhanced by 280-fold toward EGFR using the scFv antibody as shown in Figs. 10.9 and 10.10 (Zhou et al., 2007).

Review article

[Anti-infectives • New technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1471489215000995)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2015, Current Opinion in Pharmacology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/current-opinion-in-pharmacology)Hans J Stauss, ... Hinrich Abken

### Binding affinity

The interaction of the [CAR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/chimeric-antigen-receptor "Learn more about CAR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by the [antibody scFv](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/single-chain-fragment-variable-antibody "Learn more about antibody scFv from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") binding domain to target is mostly of higher affinity than binding of the physiological [TCR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/t-cell-receptor "Learn more about TCR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to peptide loaded HLA. Increasing binding affinity is thought to produce improved [T cell](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/t-cell "Learn more about T cell from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") function. However, a detailed study targeting the same epitope with different affinities revealed that CAR-mediated [T cell activation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/t-cell-activation "Learn more about T cell activation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") did not increase with the binding affinity above threshold when attacking target cells [36]; furthermore increase in affinity above threshold may result in antigen-independent T [cell activation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cell-activation "Learn more about cell activation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

In addition to the binding affinity, the position of the targeted epitope relative to the cell membrane impacts T cell activation. This became apparent when membrane distal and proximal epitopes of the same membrane-bound molecule were targeted by [CARs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chimeric-antigen-receptor "Learn more about CARs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). A higher degree of T cell activation was obtained when epitopes closer to the cell membrane were targeted [37]. This and other studies underline that membrane proximal epitopes are more potent in inducing T cell activation than distal epitopes. Independently thereof, the accessibility of the epitope for binding impacts the efficiency in CAR-mediated T cell activation.

[Insights into Enzyme Mechanisms and Functions from Experimental and Computational Methods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1876162316300293)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2016, Advances in Protein Chemistry and Structural Biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/bookseries/advances-in-protein-chemistry-and-structural-biology)D. Kilburg, E. Gallicchio

### 2.1 Statistical Mechanics Formulation of Molecular Association

From a physio-chemical perspective the noncovalent binding of two molecular species, A and B, can be thought of as a reversible chemical process at equilibrium. The [binding affinity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/binding-affinity "Learn more about binding affinity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the probability of this interaction governed by the rates of association and disassociation, _k_ on and _k_ off, respectively:

(1)A+B⇄k off k on AB

At equilibrium, the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction, and therefore the [equilibrium constant](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/equilibrium-constant "Learn more about equilibrium constant from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for binding _K_ b can be written as

(2)K b=[AB]/C 0([A]/C 0)([B]/C 0)=1 C 0 k on k off

where _C_ 0 is the standard state concentration which is often set to 1 M or 1 molecule/1668 Å 3. Eqs. (1) and (2) assume that all species involved can be treated as distinct chemical species. The standard binding free energy, Δ G b 0, which is a thermodynamic measure of the binding affinity, is defined as

(3)Δ G b 0=−k B T ln K b

where _k_ B is Boltzmann's constant. These equations, usually first introduced in undergraduate physical chemistry, are deceptive in their simplicity. The reality is that it is exceedingly difficult to compute the binding free energy between two proteins from first principles. The problem is inherently statistical in nature, having to do with the ensemble of conformations of the complex AB and their binding partners A and B when not bound. Statistical mechanics theory yields the following expression for the binding constant (Gallicchio & Levy, 2011; Gilson, Given, Bush, & McCammon, 1997)

(4)K b=C 0 8 π 2 Z N,AB Z N Z N,A Z N,B

where _Z_ _N_ is the configurational [partition function](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/partition-coefficient "Learn more about partition function from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the solvent composed of _N_ molecules and _Z_ _N_,AB, _Z_ _N_,A, and _Z_ _N_,B are the configurational partition functions of the complex, protein A, and protein B, respectively. For example, the partition function for the molecule B

(5)Z N,B=∫e−β U(x B,r s)d x B d r s

where the molecule is placed in some arbitrary position and orientation in solution and integration is carried over the 3 _n_ B − 6 internal degrees of freedom, _x_ B. _n_ B is the number of atoms in protein B, _r_ s represents the degrees of freedom of the solvent, and (U _x_ B,_r_ s) is the potential energy of the solvent/solute system. The external degrees of freedom of molecule B correspond to three translational and three rotational coordinates (collectively denoted by _ζ_ B) that specify the position and orientation of molecule B with respect to the receptor A (Boresch, Tettinger, Leitgeb, & Karplus, 2003; Gallicchio & Levy, 2011). The configurational partition function of the complex is then be written as

(6)Z N,AB=∫bound e−β U(x A,x B,ζ B,r s)d x A d x B d ζ B d r s

where the “bound” macrostate is any suitable structural definition of the complex based on the external coordinates _ζ_ B, such as the distance between the centers of mass of A and B (Gallicchio & Levy, 2012).

The statistical mechanics expression (4) forms the basis of many physics-based models of molecular binding in current use for the study of protein–drug binding equilibria (Gallicchio & Levy, 2011; Gilson et al., 1997; Guvench & MacKerell, 2009). Evaluation of (4) is daunting and complex, even for modern computers. Free energy methods (Chipot & Pohorille, 2007; Chodera et al., 2011; de Ruiter & Oostenbrink, 2011; Gallicchio, Lapelosa, & Levy, 2010) attempt to evaluate the ratio of partition functions in Eq. (4) either directly (Chang & Gilson, 2004; Kollman et al., 2000) or by free energy perturbation (FEP) approaches (Chodera et al., 2011; de Ruiter & Oostenbrink, 2011; Gallicchio et al., 2010) with varying degrees of physical rigor. Each approach comes with its pros and cons. We will discuss the most relevant of these methods as they apply to current applications in protein–peptide binding.

[Peptide, Protein and Enzyme Design](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0076687916300374)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2016, Methods in Enzymology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/bookseries/methods-in-enzymology)S.A. Hunter, J.R. Cochran

### Abstract

Determining the equilibrium-binding affinity (_K_ d) of two interacting proteins is essential not only for the biochemical study of protein signaling and function but also for the engineering of improved protein and [enzyme variants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/enzyme-variant "Learn more about enzyme variants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). One common technique for measuring protein-binding affinities uses flow cytometry to analyze [ligand binding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/ligand-binding "Learn more about ligand binding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to proteins presented on the surface of a cell. However, cell-binding assays require specific considerations to accurately quantify the [binding affinity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/binding-affinity "Learn more about binding affinity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of a protein–protein interaction. Here we will cover the basic assumptions in designing a cell-based binding assay, including the relevant equations and theory behind determining [binding affinities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/binding-affinity "Learn more about binding affinities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Further, two major considerations in measuring binding affinities—time to equilibrium and ligand depletion—will be discussed. As these conditions have the potential to greatly alter the _K_ d, methods through which to avoid or minimize them will be provided. We then outline detailed protocols for performing direct- and competitive-binding assays against proteins displayed on the surface of yeast or [mammalian cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mammalian-cell "Learn more about mammalian cells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that can be used to derive accurate _K_ d values. Finally, a comparison of cell-based binding assays to other types of binding assays will be presented.

[Affinity and Avidity of Autoantibodies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780444563781000058)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2014, Autoantibodies (Third Edition)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780444563781/autoantibodies)Borut Božič, ... Blaž Rozman

### Avidity and affinity

#### Definition

The binding of antibody with its ligand is the most important feature for the detection of (auto)antibodies and for the determination of analytes by antibodies as reagents. Antibody–antigen [intermolecular interactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/intermolecular-interaction "Learn more about intermolecular interactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are based on relatively weak physical forces. The energy of antibody–antigen binding is called affinity or [avidity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/avidity "Learn more about avidity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The molecular specificity of antibodies is also associated with the binding energy. [Antibody specificity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/antibody-specificity "Learn more about Antibody specificity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for a given antigen is determined by its relative affinity. Antibodies with very high affinity to target a specific epitope can bind to similar epitopes with lower affinity. This important aspect is not fully implemented in the analytical strategies for characterizing antibodies.

Despite their clear difference, the terms affinity and avidity are often indiscriminately used. By definition, antibody _affinity_ is the strength of binding of one antibody molecule’s [paratope](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/antibody-combining-site "Learn more about paratope from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with its corresponding epitope on the antigen molecule and can be determined only by a single monovalent [Fab fragment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/immunoglobulin-f-ab-fragment "Learn more about Fab fragment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). It is the sum of all attractive forces resulting in increased binding strength and repulsive forces resulting in decreased binding strength. The affinity constant K can be described by thermodynamic terms:

Δ G o=−RT ln K

_[Avidity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/avidity "Learn more about Avidity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ is the binding strength of all [binding sites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/binding-site "Learn more about binding sites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") between a multivalent antibody and a multivalent antigen. The measured binding energy between antibodies and their relevant, mostly complex antigens reflects the avidity of antibodies. Avidity cannot be described by thermodynamic terms, and is obtained by kinetic measurements. It is commonly indicative of the association constant, depending on the assay procedure employed. Ionic and hydrophobic interactions also increase the avidity energy, which can be tens, hundreds, or even thousands of times higher than that of affinity [2].

#### Methods of affinity calculation

Binding between antibodies and antigens is a reversible reaction that takes from a few seconds to several hours to achieve its equilibrium. The reaction may be described by the Law of [Mass Action](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mass-action "Learn more about Mass Action from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with several assumptions. The most important are that the antibody must be monovalent, recognizing only one epitope on the antigen, thus having one affinity; both reagents must be pure (homogeneous regarding to binding sites) and in a solution; and the reaction must be in equilibrium without nonspecific binding to the walls of the reaction vessel. In practice, these assumptions are not completely met. Nevertheless, the Law of Mass Action does provide a useful basis for the theoretical assessment of antibody–antigen interactions. The reaction between the monovalent antibody (Ab) and the monovalent antigen/hapten (H) can be written as follows:

Ab+H⇄k d k a AbH

where **_k_****a** is the association constant and **_k_****d** is the [dissociation constant](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/dissociation-constant "Learn more about dissociation constant from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The [equilibrium constant](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/equilibrium-constant "Learn more about equilibrium constant from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") K eq, also called the [antibody affinity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/antibody-affinity "Learn more about antibody affinity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") according to previously mentioned assumptions, represents the concentration ratio of bound to unbound monovalent antibody and [hapten](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/hapten "Learn more about hapten from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"):

K eq=k a/k d=[AbH]/[Ab][H]

After rearrangement of the calculation, a linear relationship is obtained and, graphically presented as a [Scatchard plot](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/scatchard-plot "Learn more about Scatchard plot from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This plot is useful also after reordering of the Langmuir [adsorption isotherm](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/adsorption-isotherm "Learn more about adsorption isotherm from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") equation, when binding of univalent hapten to multivalent antibody is calculated.

Theoretical background is necessary using experimental data for calculation of the equilibrium constant (affinity). Linear graphs (Scatchard or Langmuir) are obtained only theoretically. In practice, particularly dealing with polyclonal patient sera, affinity heterogeneity (and consequently avidity heterogeneity) gives a curved line, making approximations impossible. Measurements and calculations of the average association constant may be useful in such situations.

#### Methods of avidity/affinity measurement

Controversies of the role of antibodies’ avidity may be chiefly attributed to the lack of suitable measuring methods. Both affinity as well as specificity for antigens may have an important impact for the selection of a specific detection assay. There are basically three approaches: solution phase, [solid phase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/solid "Learn more about solid phase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the combination of both [3,4].

##### Solution-phase assays

According to the Law of Mass Action the very basic affinity determination comprehends methods in which an antigen–antibody interaction, as well as the separation of free molecules from the complex, always occurs in a solution:

1.
The _[equilibrium dialysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/equilibrium-dialysis "Learn more about equilibrium dialysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ to determine the affinity constant for the antibody directed to a small monovalent antigen, by measuring the concentration of free antigen (separated small antigen molecules from a larger antigen–antibody complex).

2.
_[Ultrafiltration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/ultrafiltration "Learn more about Ultrafiltration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _or_ _[ultracentrifugation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/ultracentrifugation "Learn more about ultracentrifugation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ to separate free and bound antibody, if antigens are large and multivalent. When sufficient quantities of the reagents are available and the antigen is much larger than the antibody, free antibody separated from the complex can be measured _spectrophotometrically._ When only small amounts of reagents are available, the amount of free antibody can be measured by _enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay_ (ELISA) after separating free antibody from the complexes by centrifugation.

3.
_[Equilibrium sedimentation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/sedimentation-equilibrium "Learn more about Equilibrium sedimentation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ to measure the concentration of labeled free antibody at the meniscus of the centrifuge tube when the size of an antigen is similar to that of an antibody molecule.

4.
_Labeling of antigen molecules_ to use various procedures to separate free labeled antigen from bound complexes:

a.
_precipitation_ of antibody with 50% [ammonium sulfate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/ammonium-sulfate "Learn more about ammonium sulfate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or 15% polyethylene glycol;

b.
_specific binding_ to protein A or to anti-immunoglobulin antibodies.

5.
_Spectrofluorimetric_ methods (_fluorescence quenching_, _fluorescence po

enriched_points.expanded_content: Binding affinity quantifies the strength of interaction between a drug and its target protein, influencing drug efficacy and selectivity in therapeutic applications.
Optimizing lead compounds involves modifying chemical structures to enhance binding affinity and improve pharmacokinetic properties, leading to more effective drug candidates.
Identifying the correct biological target is crucial as it ensures that the drug interacts with the intended protein, maximizing therapeutic effectiveness and minimizing side effects.
